county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Allegany County formed 1789 from Washington County, encompassing Maryland's westernmost mountain territory. County seat is Cumberland, which served as a terminus for the C&O Canal and a key stop on the National Road. Economy centered on coal mining, railroads (B&O), and timber. Predominantly Scots-Irish and German settlers.
county_metadata.research_tips: Maryland State Archives (MSA) and Allegany County Circuit Court (Cumberland). Western Maryland mountain county formed 1789 from Washington County. Cumberland was a gateway to the west via the National Road (1811) and the C&O Canal. Land records at mdlandrec.net from 1789. Allegany County had significant coal mining and railroad employment — check B&O Railroad employee records at the B&O Railroad Museum in Baltimore. German and Scots-Irish settlers predominated. The Western Maryland Regional Library holds local histories and newspaper indexes. Fort Cumberland records at MSA document the French and Indian War era.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: Allegany land records on mdlandrec.net; MSA has digitized deed books, mortgages, and land patents; one of the most complete online land record systems in the US

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Anne Arundel County formed 1650 as one of Maryland's original counties. County seat is Annapolis, the state capital since 1694 and home to the US Naval Academy (1845). Located on the western shore of the Chesapeake Bay. Tobacco was the primary cash crop through the colonial era. One of Maryland's most populous counties historically.
county_metadata.research_tips: Maryland State Archives (MSA) in Annapolis and Anne Arundel County Circuit Court. As the state capital county, Anne Arundel has excellent records — MSA is physically located here. One of the oldest counties (1650), records include colonial-era land patents, court proceedings, and probate. The US Naval Academy in Annapolis (1845) generated military personnel records. Extensive tobacco plantation records document enslaved persons. Land records on mdlandrec.net from the 1650s. St. Anne's Episcopal Church (1692) and other colonial parishes have vital records predating civil registration. The Anne Arundel County Historical Society has local genealogical resources.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: Anne Arundel land records on mdlandrec.net; MSA has digitized deed books, mortgages, and land patents; one of the most complete online land record systems in the US

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Baltimore County formed 1659 from Anne Arundel County. County seat is Towson. The county originally included Baltimore Town, which became an independent city in 1851. The county now completely surrounds Baltimore City but is a wholly separate jurisdiction with its own court system and records. Pre-1851 records cover both the city and county.
county_metadata.research_tips: MSA and Baltimore County Circuit Court (Towson). Not to be confused with Baltimore City — the county completely surrounds the independent city but is a separate jurisdiction since 1851. Pre-1851 records cover both city and county. Land records on mdlandrec.net. Baltimore County was one of the original counties (1659) and at one time encompassed much of northern Maryland. The Baltimore County Historical Society in Cockeysville holds local records. German settlement in the northern part of the county (Hereford, Parkton). Iron furnace records document early industrial workers. Check MSA for colonial-era records and the county library system for local history collections.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: Baltimore land records on mdlandrec.net; MSA has digitized deed books, mortgages, and land patents; one of the most complete online land record systems in the US

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Baltimore City became independent from Baltimore County in 1851, functioning as a separate jurisdiction equivalent to a county with FIPS code 24510. It is Maryland's largest city and was one of the nation's major ports. The city has its own circuit court, land records office, and register of wills. Pre-1851 records are shared with Baltimore County at MSA. Baltimore had the largest free Black population of any US city before the Civil War.
county_metadata.research_tips: MSA, Baltimore City Circuit Court, and the Maryland Center for History and Culture (formerly Maryland Historical Society). Baltimore City became independent from Baltimore County in 1851. Pre-1851 records are shared with Baltimore County at MSA. Baltimore was a major immigration port — passenger arrival lists from the 1820s are at NARA (M255, M596) and on Ancestry/FamilySearch. The Enoch Pratt Free Library's Maryland Department holds city directories from the 1790s, newspapers, and maps. Baltimore had the largest free Black population of any American city before the Civil War — check free negro registers at MSA, AME church records, and the Afro-American newspaper archives. Catholic parish records are extensive given Maryland's founding as a Catholic colony. The Archdiocese of Baltimore Archives holds sacramental records from the 1790s.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: Baltimore City land records on mdlandrec.net from 1851 (when city became independent); pre-1851 records under Baltimore County; MSA has digitized virtually all land records

source_profiles.source_name: Baltimore (city) Land Records -- Maryland State Archives / mdlandrec.net

county_metadata.record_losses: 1882 courthouse fire destroyed many early records including deed books, court minutes, and wills prior to that date
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Calvert County formed 1654 as one of the original Maryland counties, initially called Patuxent County. County seat is Prince Frederick. BURNED COUNTY: The 1882 courthouse fire destroyed many early records. Southern Maryland tobacco county on the western shore of the Chesapeake Bay. Named for the Calvert family (Lords Baltimore), the proprietors of Maryland.
county_metadata.research_tips: MSA and Calvert County Circuit Court (Prince Frederick). BURNED COUNTY: The 1882 courthouse fire destroyed many early records including deeds, wills, and court minutes. Researchers must use MSA colonial records, church records, and state-level copies to reconstruct pre-1882 genealogies. One of the original counties (1654, as Patuxent County). Southern Maryland tobacco plantation county with significant enslaved population documented in remaining tax lists and estate records. Calvert County Historical Society has published transcriptions of surviving records. Land records on mdlandrec.net begin after the fire. Check All Hallows Episcopal Church (1692) and Christ Church records for vital events predating civil registration.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: Calvert County land records on mdlandrec.net; post-1882 records are complete; pre-fire records partially reconstructed from state-level copies at MSA

source_profiles.survival_notes: Post-fire records survive; pre-fire land records partially reconstructed from state-level copies

source_profiles.survival_notes: Many early probate records destroyed in 1882 courthouse fire; some reconstructed from MSA copies

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Caroline County formed 1774 from Dorchester and Queen Anne's counties, one of the last counties formed before the Revolution. County seat is Denton. Eastern Shore agricultural county. Named for Lady Caroline Eden, wife of Maryland's last colonial governor.
county_metadata.research_tips: MSA and Caroline County Circuit Court (Denton). Eastern Shore county formed 1774 from Dorchester and Queen Anne's — check parent counties for earlier records. Land records on mdlandrec.net from 1774. Caroline County was predominantly agricultural with tobacco and grain farming. The county had a significant enslaved population — estate inventories and tax assessments at MSA document enslaved persons by name. Harriet Tubman's family had connections to Caroline County through the adjacent Dorchester County networks. Check Denton area church records, especially Methodist and Episcopal parishes. The Caroline County Historical Society maintains local genealogical files.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: Caroline land records on mdlandrec.net; MSA has digitized deed books, mortgages, and land patents; one of the most complete online land record systems in the US

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Carroll County formed 1837 from Baltimore and Frederick counties. County seat is Westminster. Piedmont agricultural county with strong German heritage, particularly German Reformed and Lutheran communities. Named for Charles Carroll of Carrollton, last surviving signer of the Declaration of Independence.
county_metadata.research_tips: MSA and Carroll County Circuit Court (Westminster). Formed 1837 from Baltimore and Frederick counties — check both parents for pre-1837 records. Land records on mdlandrec.net from 1837. Strongly German-influenced county in the Piedmont region. The Historical Society of Carroll County in Westminster has extensive local records including family files, cemetery transcriptions, and photographs. German Reformed, Lutheran, and Church of the Brethren (Dunker) records are important sources. Western Maryland College (now McDaniel College) archives hold local papers. Carroll County was a center of Unionism during the Civil War despite its border-state location.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: Carroll land records on mdlandrec.net; MSA has digitized deed books, mortgages, and land patents; one of the most complete online land record systems in the US

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Cecil County formed 1674 from Baltimore and Kent counties. County seat is Elkton, located at the head of the Chesapeake Bay near the Pennsylvania border. The Susquehanna and Elk Rivers provided transportation. Elkton became famous as a 'marriage mill' destination. Named for Cecil Calvert, 2nd Baron Baltimore.
county_metadata.research_tips: MSA and Cecil County Circuit Court (Elkton). Formed 1674 from Baltimore and Kent counties. Located at the head of the Chesapeake Bay with connections to both Maryland and Pennsylvania. Elkton was famous as a 'marriage mill' — thousands of out-of-state couples married there from the 1800s through the 1930s due to no waiting period, making marriage records unusually rich. Land records on mdlandrec.net from the 1670s. Quaker settlement around Nottingham and Rising Sun — check Quaker meeting records at Swarthmore College. The Historical Society of Cecil County holds local records. Iron furnace and canal records document industrial workers along the Susquehanna and C&D Canal corridor.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: Cecil land records on mdlandrec.net; MSA has digitized deed books, mortgages, and land patents; one of the most complete online land record systems in the US

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Charles County formed 1658 as one of the original Maryland counties. County seat moved from Port Tobacco to La Plata in 1895. Southern Maryland tobacco county with extensive plantation economy. Strong Catholic heritage due to Jesuit missions. Named for Charles Calvert, 3rd Baron Baltimore.
county_metadata.research_tips: MSA and Charles County Circuit Court (La Plata). One of the original counties (1658) in Southern Maryland. Rich colonial and tobacco plantation records at MSA. Significant enslaved population — Charles County slave records are among the most detailed in Maryland. Land records on mdlandrec.net from the 1650s. Catholic parish records are particularly important here — St. Thomas Manor (Jesuit, 1637) is one of the oldest Catholic communities in English-speaking America. Thomas Stone, Declaration signer, was from Charles County. The Charles County Community College Southern Maryland Studies Center holds local history collections. Port Tobacco was the colonial county seat before La Plata.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: Charles land records on mdlandrec.net; MSA has digitized deed books, mortgages, and land patents; one of the most complete online land record systems in the US

source_profiles.source_name: Charles Land Records -- Maryland State Archives / mdlandrec.net
source_profiles.repository: Maryland State Archives, Annapolis / mdlandrec.net
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Land records for Charles on mdlandrec.net; digitized deed books, mortgages, and land patents; MSA has indexed and imaged virtually all Maryland land records online through mdlandrec.net, one of the best state land record systems in the US

source_profiles.source_name: Charles Probate Records -- MSA / Register of Wills / Orphans' Court
source_profiles.repository: Maryland State Archives, Annapolis / Charles Register of Wills
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Wills, inventories, administrations, and estate accounts for Charles; Orphans' Court handled probate, guardianships, and apprenticeships; records at MSA and county Register of Wills

source_profiles.source_name: Charles Federal Census Records
source_profiles.repository: NARA / FamilySearch / Ancestry
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Federal census 1790-1950 for Charles; Maryland was enumerated in every federal census; 1790 census is complete for Maryland; slave schedules available for 1850 and 1860

source_profiles.source_name: Charles Marriage Records -- MSA / Clerk of Circuit Court
source_profiles.repository: Maryland State Archives, Annapolis / Charles Clerk of Circuit Court
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Marriage licenses and records for Charles; earlier marriages found in church records; FamilySearch has indexed many Maryland marriages; MSA holds county marriage records

source_profiles.source_name: Charles County Catholic and Church Records -- Jesuit / St. Thomas Manor
source_profiles.repository: Georgetown University Archives / Archdiocese of Baltimore / Maryland State Archives
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Jesuit records from St. Thomas Manor (1637), one of the oldest Catholic communities in English-speaking America; sacramental records including baptisms, marriages, and burials; Georgetown University Archives hold Jesuit plantation records documenting both free families and enslaved persons

source_profiles.source_name: Charles County Enslaved Persons Records -- MSA / Georgetown University
source_profiles.repository: Maryland State Archives / Georgetown University Archives
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Charles County had one of the largest enslaved populations in Maryland; estate inventories, slave sales, manumission records, and Jesuit plantation records at Georgetown University document enslaved families in detail; the 1838 Jesuit slave sale sent 272 persons to Louisiana

source_profiles.source_name: Charles County Tax Records -- MSA
source_profiles.repository: Maryland State Archives, Annapolis
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Colonial and post-colonial tax assessments listing planters, property, tobacco production, and enslaved persons; Port Tobacco district records detail the colonial county seat area

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Dorchester County formed 1669 from Talbot County. County seat is Cambridge on the Eastern Shore. Birthplace of Harriet Tubman (c. 1822). Economy based on agriculture, timber, fishing, and oystering. Significant enslaved and free Black population. Named for the Earl of Dorset.
county_metadata.research_tips: MSA and Dorchester County Circuit Court (Cambridge). Eastern Shore county formed 1669 from Talbot. Birthplace of Harriet Tubman (born Araminta Ross, c. 1822) — the Harriet Tubman Underground Railroad Visitor Center and Tubman Byway document her family's history. Land records on mdlandrec.net from the 1660s. Dorchester County had a large enslaved population and a significant free Black community. Timber, fishing, and agriculture were key industries. The Dorchester County Historical Society in Cambridge holds local records. Old Trinity Church (Episcopal, 1692) has colonial-era parish records. Check MSA for Dorchester manumission records and free negro certificates.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: Dorchester land records on mdlandrec.net; MSA has digitized deed books, mortgages, and land patents; one of the most complete online land record systems in the US

source_profiles.source_name: Dorchester County Church Records -- Old Trinity, Methodist
source_profiles.repository: Maryland State Archives / Episcopal Diocese of Maryland / Local parishes
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Old Trinity Episcopal Church (1692) colonial parish records; Methodist circuit records from the late 1700s; essential for pre-1898 vital events; Bucktown area church records may relate to Tubman family history

source_profiles.source_name: Dorchester County Enslaved Persons and African American Records -- MSA
source_profiles.repository: Maryland State Archives, Annapolis / Harriet Tubman Underground Railroad Visitor Center
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Dorchester County was central to the Underground Railroad. Enslaved persons records include estate inventories, manumission records, free negro certificates, and runaway advertisements. The Tubman family's records have been extensively researched; MSA's Legacy of Slavery database includes Dorchester records
